narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic journey have been carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts performance on the bar exam to ensure we select students likely to succeed on their first try. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, a Year 3 decile of 1, an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, an LSAT score of 28, middle family income, and plans to attend full-time—solid foundations from which you've built your path. The model estimates a 95.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam on the first attempt, a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass. This high failure risk stems primarily from three key factors in your record, ranked by their influence on the prediction. Most significantly, your Year 3 decile ranking of 1—well below the typical student's 5.53 average—strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure, signaling lower performance relative to peers in your later undergraduate years. Next, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the average of 5.71, further reinforced this direction, indicating early academic struggles that carried weight in the assessment. Your LSAT score of 28, lower than the dataset average of 36.7, also contributed toward the failure prediction, though to a lesser degree than your deciles. While your middle income and full-time plans align with most applicants and didn't sway things much, these top academic indicators painted a picture riskier than that of typical admitted students, who generally show stronger decile rankings and test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience as a Hispanic man pursuing law—many overcome similar starts—but the model focuses on patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying once you've addressed these core areas. Schools value growth stories like yours, and targeted improvements can shift the odds dramatically in your favor. Keep pushing; your determination will open doors.
